title: Module 5 quiz — Strategy, Hiring, and Ongoing SEO

questions.prompt: A founder asks you whether to invest in SEO. Their business is pure outbound B2B sales — every deal comes from cold outreach or referrals. Their customers don't search Google to find solutions like theirs. What's the right answer?

choices.text: Always invest in SEO — every business needs it.

SEO is at best a low priority for them. The first decision question is whether customers actually use Google to find businesses like yours. If not, paid acquisition / outbound / referrals are where to invest. Revisit SEO if the GTM motion changes.
Invest $10K/month minimum — they have to compete.
Only invest if they're in a tech niche.

questions.explanation: Channel fit comes first. SEO is high-leverage when customers search Google for what you offer. For pure outbound B2B sales-led companies, that's often not how deals happen. SEO won't fix a channel-fit problem. Invest where the customers actually are.

questions.prompt: In Search Console, which report should you use to find queries where you have lots of impressions but very low CTR?

choices.text: Page Indexing report.
Core Web Vitals report.
Performance report — filter by query and look at the impressions/CTR columns. Low-CTR queries with high impressions usually mean your title and meta description aren't compelling for those queries.
Manual actions report.

questions.explanation: Performance is the right report. High impressions + low CTR is the classic signal that your title link and meta description aren't doing their job for those queries. Rewriting them is one of the highest-leverage quick wins available.

questions.prompt: An SEO firm proposes a contract that includes 'creating supporting sites that will redirect to yours.' They will register the domains 'on your behalf.' Should you proceed?

choices.text: Yes — more domains, more authority.

No. This is a classic 'shadow domain' scam Google explicitly warns about. The firm retains domain ownership; if you part ways, they redirect the domains to a competitor. It's also a likely spam-policy violation. Walk away. If you must allow it, you (not the firm) register and own every domain.
Only if they're under $50 per domain.
Only if the domains have keywords in them.

questions.explanation: Google's 'Do you need an SEO?' doc explicitly lists shadow domains as a classic scam. The leverage dynamic is bad, the spam risk is real, and the redirect-funnel tactic itself violates spam policies. Walk away — and never let an SEO register domains that should belong to you.

questions.prompt: Traffic dropped 50% on a Tuesday with no visible Google update announced. What's the smart first move?

choices.text: Disavow all your backlinks just in case.

First, define the drop precisely: what metric, what date, what pages and queries are affected. Then check for a technical regression — was there a deploy? Is robots.txt healthy? Did pages start returning errors? Did a noindex tag accidentally ship? Page Indexing in Search Console is the fastest sitewide check.
Roll back all changes from the last 3 months.
Hire a recovery agency immediately.

questions.explanation: The diagnostic order matters. Define the drop precisely first. Then rule out technical regressions before assuming algorithm updates or content quality issues — they're the fastest to find and the most common cause of sudden drops. Reflexively disavowing links or rolling back content usually hurts more than helps.

questions.prompt: How long does Google say it typically takes to see SEO results from changes you make to your site?

choices.text: A few hours.
1–2 weeks.
4 months to a year.
Less than 30 days.

questions.explanation: Direct from Google: 'It will take time for you to see results: typically from four months to a year from the time you begin making changes until you start to see the benefits.' This is why SEO and paid acquisition aren't substitutes — SEO is a compounding investment with a long lead time, not a way to fix a revenue gap this quarter.
